# Introduction {.unnumbered}

This is a personal, curated reference on research-design and inference methods —
the place I write things down once, properly, so I do not have to re-derive them
each time they come up in my own work or in refereeing someone else's. It is
opinionated by design: where the literature offers a menu, I record the choice I
would actually defend and why, not a neutral survey.

## How it is organised

The book has two kinds of chapter, and they are read differently.

- **Long, self-contained chapters** develop one topic from intuition to working
  practice. They are meant to be read start-to-finish at least once, then dipped
  back into. The [Estimands primer](estimands-primer.qmd) is the first of these:
  what an estimand is, the taxonomy, where intuition breaks, how designs and
  sampling pin down the target, and how to write and critique estimands in real
  papers. It carries interactive checkpoints, worked R, and a running
  coastal-flooding example.

- **[Methods Miscellany](misc-methods.qmd)** collects *short, independent notes* —
  one self-contained section per question, each readable on its own. It is where
  a useful idea lives until (and unless) it grows into a chapter of its own. It
  currently holds two notes: one on the **ecological fallacy** (is cross-level
  inference a statistical, an estimand, or a mechanisms problem?), and one on
  **weighting** in political science (do we use it too little, and which methods
  are actually promising?).

As the collection grows, expect more long chapters and, very likely, more than
one miscellany — split by area (causal inference, measurement, text-as-data) once
any single one gets unwieldy.

## Conventions

British spelling throughout. Code is R unless stated otherwise, and every code
block shows its computed output inline. Cross-references are live: a mention of a
section elsewhere in the book is a link. Where a claim leans on a specific result,
the source is named in the text so it can be traced.
